category_title: Probationary high school students with severe behavioral challenges and resistance to instruction
minimal_context: The student is asked to read from the history textbook about the Industrial Age and World War I in the classroom.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Please open your history textbooks and read the chapter on the Industrial Age and World War I.

STUDENT: (Tears a page out of the textbook) Why are you teaching us bad kids?

TUTOR: Please leave the book intact and focus on the reading.

STUDENT: (Throws a paper ball across the room) I hate the subject of history. We don't pay attention while reading.

TUTOR: We need to finish this lesson. Keep trying to read.

STUDENT: (Squirts hand sanitizer on the chair and tries to set it on fire) I'm doing an unauthorized science experiment.

options.A: Concentrate on the long-term teaching of a small repertoire of reading strategies (predicting, questioning, imagining, clarifying, and summarizing) beginning with teacher explanation and modeling, with gradual release of responsibility.
options.B: Establish a consistent two-week routine for every history unit, maintaining a predictable schedule for textbook reading assignments and classroom activities throughout the duration of the course.
options.C: Adhere to an established set of rules and penalties regarding student behavior in the classroom, starting with a warning, followed by a ten-minute timeout, and then increasingly serious disciplinary measures.
options.D: Instruct the student to trace images from the history textbook, search related images online, choose pictures of high interest, discuss the stories behind them, and re-create artifacts of their own.
